name: Jacob S. Coxey
description: Ohio quarry owner, Greenback-Labor populist, and originator of the modern protest march on Washington. In response to the catastrophic unemployment of the Panic of 1893, Coxey organized the 'Commonweal of Christ' — popularly called Coxey's Army — and led roughly 500 unemployed men on foot from Massillon, Ohio beginning March 25, 1894 to demand a federal $500 million good-roads program funded by non-interest-bearing currency. The march reached Washington on April 30. On May 1, 1894, attempting to deliver his 'petition in boots' from the Capitol steps, Coxey was arrested by Capitol Police along with chief lieutenant Carl Browne and Philadelphia delegate Christopher Columbus Jones. The trio was charged with carrying banners on the Capitol grounds and walking on Capitol grass. Convicted May 8, 1894 in police court before Judge Thomas Miller; sentenced May 21, 1894 to 20 days in the District of Columbia jail and a $5 fine. Released June 10, 1894. The arrests provoked national outrage and made the right to demonstrate on the Capitol grounds a pivotal civil-liberties question. Coxey continued advocating for federal jobs programs for the next forty years; he eventually delivered his unread 1894 speech from the Capitol steps in 1944, on its 50th anniversary, by congressional invitation.

cases.charges: Carrying banners and walking on the U.S. Capitol grounds (May 1, 1894 march)
cases.sentence: 20 days in the D.C. jail and $5 fine

name: Carl Browne
description: California labor agitator, illustrator, and chief lieutenant of Coxey's Army — known within the Commonweal of Christ as 'the Marshal.' A veteran of California Workingmen's Party agitation in the 1870s and a Greenback-Labor party stalwart, Browne joined Jacob Coxey in Massillon, Ohio in early 1894 to organize the 'petition in boots' march on Washington. He designed the Army's banners, uniforms, and ritual; preached the 'Reincarnation of Christ' theology of the Commonweal; and managed the daily logistics of the 500-man column on its 35-day walk to D.C. Arrested with Coxey and Christopher Columbus Jones at the U.S. Capitol on May 1, 1894 for carrying banners on the Capitol grounds and walking on Capitol grass. Convicted May 8, 1894 in police court before Judge Thomas Miller; sentenced May 21, 1894 to 20 days in the District of Columbia jail. Released June 10, 1894. He continued organizing labor protests until his death in 1914.

cases.charges: Carrying banners and walking on the U.S. Capitol grounds (May 1, 1894 Coxey's Army march)

name: Christopher Columbus Jones
description: Philadelphia carpenter and Greenback-Labor activist who led the small Philadelphia contingent of Coxey's Army to its rendezvous with Jacob Coxey and Carl Browne in Washington, D.C. in late April 1894. On May 1, 1894 he marched with Coxey and Browne up the Capitol steps to deliver the Commonweal of Christ's petition for a federal good-roads jobs program; all three were arrested by Capitol Police for carrying banners and walking on the Capitol grass. Convicted May 8, 1894 in D.C. police court before Judge Thomas Miller; sentenced May 21, 1894 to 20 days in the District of Columbia jail. Released June 10, 1894.

cases.charges: Carrying banners and walking on the U.S. Capitol grounds (May 1, 1894 Coxey's Army march)
